dimensions.summary: AGENTS.md exists but is minimal (102 chars) with only basic warnings about money math, test discipline, and webhook verification. No architecture map, no build/test/run command documentation, no prompt libraries or advanced techniques. Agents have little structured guidance to follow.
strengths: AGENTS.md acknowledges critical domain concerns (money math, signatures)
Brief guidance is focused on high-risk areas
gaps: No architecture overview or team conventions documented
No build/test/run workflows explained for agents
No MCP config, copilot-instructions, or prompt libraries
No advanced techniques (subagents, hooks, skills, tool-permission policy)

dimensions.summary: Vitest test suite is active and behavioral. tests/checkout.test.ts contains 3 meaningful test cases covering edge cases (float drift in money math, refund overflow validation, partial refund logic). Test script is configured in package.json and CI includes build/lint. Suite is small but demonstrates solid test discipline for core money-handling logic.
strengths: Behavioral tests for critical domain logic (refund validation, money math)
Edge-case coverage (float drift, refund overflow)
Vitest framework properly configured and runnable via pnpm test
Test-to-source ratio is reasonable for a focused service
gaps: No e2e or integration tests visible (API/webhook flows untested)
No coverage reporting or coverage thresholds configured
Test suite is small relative to codebase size
No accessibility, performance, or contract-testing signals

dimensions.summary: GitHub Actions ci.yml present with build and lint steps, but test execution is absent from the sampled workflow and no deploy/release automation exists. README notes deployment is manual (build container, push ECR, update ECS). Pipeline gates on linting/building but not on tests, and no semantic-release, preview deploys, or progressive delivery patterns.
strengths: CI config present and committed (.github/workflows/ci.yml)
Build and lint steps are automated
Branch protection requires PR approval before merge
pnpm dependency lock is enforced (--frozen-lockfile)
gaps: Test step missing from ci.yml despite vitest being configured
No deploy or release workflow (manual ECS updates)
No semantic-release, changesets, or release automation
No preview deploys, IaC, or progressive delivery (Argo/Flagger)

dimensions.summary: No agentic workflows are evident. No AI code-review bots (CodeRabbit, Claude review), no auto-fix or auto-PR tooling, no Renovate auto-merge. AI-involved PRs (30% rate) lack systematic governance. This is a manual, keyboard-assist-only environment.
strengths: Structured PR review discipline (1 approval required)
gaps: No AI code-review automation (CodeRabbit, Greptile, etc.)
No auto-fix or auto-PR tooling
No dependency auto-merge (Renovate/Dependabot auto-merge)
AI-involved PRs have 50% review rate — inconsistent governance

dimensions.summary: README is substantial (4.2k chars) and covers setup, testing, and deployment in plain language. docs/ contains 3 markdown files. Architecture is clear from code (Express, Zod, Stripe-backed). Missing: ADRs, CONTRIBUTING.md, detailed API documentation, and inline doc density is not visible.
strengths: README covers all essential workflows (setup, test, deploy)
Architecture is clearly stated and understandable
docs/ directory signals commitment to documentation
Clear language suitable for new developers
gaps: No ADRs or decision-log docs (docs/adr/)
No CONTRIBUTING.md or developer onboarding guide
No OpenAPI/Swagger or API documentation
No inline code density visible (methods lack JSDoc comments)

dimensions.summary: ESLint and TypeScript strict mode are configured and enforced. tsconfig has strict=true. No pre-commit hooks (husky/lint-staged) to shift checks left. No commitlint for conventional-commit enforcement, no CODEOWNERS file. Guardrails are present but incomplete and not enforced at commit time.
strengths: ESLint configured (signal confirms present)
TypeScript strict mode enabled (tsconfig strict=true)
Type checking is enforced in build step
70% conventional-commit adoption shows discipline
gaps: No pre-commit hooks (.pre-commit-config, husky) to catch issues before push
No commitlint configuration to enforce conventional commits universally
No CODEOWNERS file for domain-based review assignment
Guardrails are tooling-only, not CI-enforced

dimensions.summary: 42 commits in 30 days shows healthy velocity. Conventional-commit rate is ~70%, indicating developer discipline. Small-PR rate is 52% (≤200 lines), signaling good batching. However, no AI co-author trailers (Co-Authored-By: Claude/Copilot) are visible in the recent sample, suggesting minimal AI-native workflow in commit history.
strengths: 42 commits in 30 days indicates active, sustained development
~70% conventional-commit rate shows team discipline
52% small-PR rate (≤200 lines) demonstrates thoughtful batching
Median time-to-merge of 26h suggests healthy review velocity
gaps: No AI co-author trailers in commit history (no evidence of AI-generated code workflows)
30% of PRs lack conventional-commit prefixes
No bot-authored commits visible (no automation driving changes)
Commit message depth not visible (signals quality of narrative)

dimensions.summary: main branch is protected with PR requirement and 1 mandatory approval. 65% of PRs are reviewed, and merge rate is healthy (78%). However, AI-involved PRs (30%) have only 50% review rate, signaling weak AI governance. No code-owner review, no signatures, no evals/golden tests, and no agent-readable operational docs or issue/PR templates.
strengths: main branch protection is enforced (requires PR + 1 approval)
65% of merged PRs have approving review
Median time-to-first-review of 5h shows responsive review culture
38 of 96 PRs analyzed provides good signal sample
gaps: AI-involved PRs (30%) have only 50% review rate — inconsistent governance
No code-owner review rules to assign domain expertise
No commit signatures or artifact signing
No evals, golden tests, or structured AI verification harness

dimensions.summary: dependabot.yml is configured for dependency scanning. However, 3 of 5 GitHub Actions are unpinned (use @v4 tag, not commit SHAs), creating supply-chain risk. No SAST (CodeQL, Semgrep), no secret scanning (gitleaks), no SBOM generation, no artifact signing, and no SECURITY.md policy. Supply-chain security is minimal.
strengths: dependabot.yml present for dependency updates
GitHub Actions are mostly pinned to @v4 (partial compliance)
gaps: 3 of 5 GitHub Actions unpinned (use @v4, not SHAs) — supply-chain risk
No SAST tooling (CodeQL, Semgrep, SonarQube)
No secret scanning (gitleaks, trufflehog, detect-secrets)
No SBOM, artifact signing, or SECURITY.md policy

headline: Solid test discipline and CI fundamentals on a small payment service team, but agent guidance is minimal and autonomous workflows are absent—limiting trust in AI-driven development.
strengths: Behavioral test coverage for critical money-math logic demonstrates domain-aware testing discipline
Healthy PR velocity (26h median time-to-merge) and small-batch cadence (52% of PRs ≤200 lines) show sustainable development practices
70% conventional-commit adoption and 1.1 reviews/PR indicate team process maturity
TypeScript strict mode and ESLint enforced provide guardrails for type safety and code consistency
Clear README and architecture make the service easy to understand for new developers
risks: AI-governed PRs (30% AI-involved, but only 50% reviewed) lack consistent oversight—risky for a payment service
Test execution is missing from the CI workflow despite vitest being configured, creating a gap between local discipline and automated gates
Agent guidance (AGENTS.md) is too thin to reliably steer autonomous AI workflows; agents lack architecture map and build/test/run playbooks
Manual deployment (no release automation) and unpinned GitHub Actions (3 of 5) create supply-chain friction and risk
No pre-commit hooks mean code-quality checks are not shifted left, reducing developer feedback speed

roadmap.title: Integrate test execution into the CI pipeline to gate merges on passing tests
roadmap.rationale: Tests exist and are behavioral, but ci.yml doesn't run them—defeating the point of CI. Adding `pnpm test` to the build step closes a critical verification gap and gates merges on test health.
explore: Is there a reason the test step was omitted from ci.yml, or was it overlooked?
Would adding `pnpm test` to the build job (after lint, before deploy) give you confidence to gate releases?

roadmap.title: Enrich AGENTS.md with architecture, build/test/run workflows, and constraints so agents have a shared playbook
roadmap.rationale: AGENTS.md is a stub—only 3 sentences. Agents driving PRs (30% AI-involved rate) need documented architecture, explicit test-after-change discipline, build/dev/test commands, and domain constraints (money math, Stripe webhooks). This is the difference between ad hoc and systemic AI adoption.
explore: What patterns or pitfalls do your best developers know about this service that agents should too?
If you wrote a runbook for 'onboarding a new agent to this codebase,' what would it cover?

roadmap.title: Establish a code-owner review rule and consistent AI-PR governance to raise the bar for AI-involved changes
roadmap.rationale: 30% of PRs involve AI, but only 50% are reviewed—below your overall 65% review rate. For a payment service, AI-generated code is high-stakes. Code-owner rules (via CODEOWNERS) and a Definition-of-Done for AI-PRs (e.g., always require a second review, always test) would make governance explicit and trustworthy.
explore: Which domains (money math, webhooks, API endpoints) deserve extra review rigor from AI-generated changes?
Would a checklist (tests pass, [domain owner approves], no secrets) for AI-PRs help raise confidence?

roadmap.title: Add pre-commit hooks to shift linting and type checks left, reducing friction in the review loop
roadmap.rationale: ESLint and TypeScript are configured but run only in CI. Pre-commit hooks (husky + lint-staged) catch issues before push, speeding up local feedback and reducing back-and-forth reviews. This is especially valuable for AI-assisted PRs, which benefit from tight feedback loops.
explore: Do developers currently run lint/type-check locally before pushing, or do they rely on CI feedback?
Would a husky setup that runs `pnpm lint` and `tsc` on commit improve your review velocity?

roadmap.title: Add SAST (CodeQL) and secret scanning (gitleaks) to the CI pipeline to shift supply-chain checks into automation
roadmap.rationale: dependabot covers dependencies, but SAST (CodeQL) and secret scanning are free GitHub Actions that detect code vulnerabilities and leaked credentials before merge. For a payment service handling Stripe keys, this is a low-effort, high-value addition.
explore: Have you had any incidents with leaked secrets or code vulnerabilities that escaped CI?
Would adding GitHub's CodeQL (SAST) + gitleaks workflows give you more confidence in merges?

discrepancies.claim: Signal reported no test files (signalScore=22), but tests/checkout.test.ts is sampled in the evidence with 3 vitest test cases covering behavioral and edge-case logic (float drift, refund overflow, partial refund). Test script is also in package.json.
